explanation: The statement "prop_0 when the autopilot is prop_1 (not request) and prop_2 (not limits)" means that prop_0 must be true whenever the conditions 'not request' and 'not limits' are met. This translates to a global implication: G((!prop_1 & !prop_2) -> prop_0).

The keyword "when" indicates a global implication. It means that whenever the condition (prop_1 and prop_2) holds, prop_0 must also hold at that very moment. This translates to a global implication: G( (prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0 ). The parenthetical notes like "(not request)" and "(not limits)" are descriptive annotations for the propositions and are not translated into logical conditions themselves.

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must also be true. This universal truth over time is best expressed using the global operator 'G' combined with an implication.

The statement "The sensor shall prop_0 when the autopilot is prop_1ing support (prop_1) and limits are not exceeded (prop_2)" translates to a global implication. It means that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must also be true. The 'shall' indicates that this condition must always hold, which is represented by the 'G' (globally) operator.

The phrase 'A when B and C' translates to 'Globally, if B and C are true, then A is true'. In this case, 'prop_0' is A, and 'prop_1 and prop_2' is B and C. The parenthetical notes 'not request' and 'not limits' are descriptive labels for the propositions and do not introduce additional logical conditions or negations. Therefore, the LTL formula expresses that 'prop_0' must always be true whenever 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are both true.

The statement "prop_0 when the autopilot is not prop_1ing support (not prop_1) and prop_2 are not exceeded (not prop_2)" can be rephrased as "if (the autopilot is not prop_1ing support) AND (prop_2 are not exceeded), then prop_0 is true". 'Not prop_1ing support' translates to '!prop_1', and 'prop_2 are not exceeded' translates to '!prop_2'. Therefore, the condition is `!prop_1 & !prop_2`. The implication 'when' or 'if...then' connects this condition to `prop_0`, resulting in `(!prop_1 & !prop_2) -> prop_0`.

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that at any point in time, if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true at that same point. This is a global implication, which is best captured by the G (Globally) operator in LTL.

The statement "The sensor shall prop_0 when X and Y" translates to a global implication: G((X & Y) -> prop_0). Here, X is "the autopilot is not prop_1ing support (not prop_1)" which is `!prop_1`, and Y is "prop_2 are not exceeded (not prop_2)" which is `!prop_2`. Combining these gives G((!prop_1 & !prop_2) -> prop_0).

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" indicates that prop_0 is true whenever the conditions prop_1 and prop_2 are both met. This is a general rule that should hold at all times, hence the use of the global operator (G) for "always". The conditions 'not request' and 'not limits' are descriptive labels for prop_1 and prop_2 respectively, not separate propositions or negations to be added to the LTL.

The statement 'prop_0 when A and B' translates to 'If A and B are true, then prop_0 is true'. In this case, A is 'the autopilot is prop_1 (not request)' which is represented by 'prop_1', and B is 'prop_2 (not limits)' which is represented by 'prop_2'. Therefore, the LTL formula is (prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0, meaning if both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 must also hold at the same time.
